coredata.dc:title: Novel carbon quantum dots enhanced carbon nanotubes-graphene hybrid nanocomposite for VOCs detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Hejazi, Mohamad-Anas
Coşkun, İbrahim Yağız
Ünlü, Fatma Yelda
Aydogan, Abdullah
Ünlü, Caner
Trabzon, Levent
coredata.dc:description: In recent years, researchers have shown a significant interest in exploring carbon-based nanomaterials for their potential applications in sensing technologies. This study introduces the development of an innovative composite material through the integration of quantum dots with carbon nanotubes and graphene. This nanohybrid composite serves as a sensing material for detecting ethanol at room temperature. The carbon quantum dots (CQD) used in this study were synthesized through a solvothermal process and integrated with carbon nanotubes (CNT) and graphene nanoplates (GNP) to investigate the synergistic effects on the composite's structure and its sensing properties. The resulting hybrid composite was applied as a sensitive film onto the surface of a quartz crystal microbalance (QCM) sensor. The hybrid nanocomposite-based sensor demonstrated significantly enhanced sensing sensitivity. At an ethanol concentration of 500 ppm, the CQD-enhanced CNT-GNP composite exhibited approximately 10- and 15-fold higher responses compared to CNT- and GNP-coated sensors, respectively. The response/recovery times of the CQD-enhanced CNT-GNP composite sensor were approximately 2/0.5 min, respectively. The sensor maintained reasonable repeatability and good recovery. The findings highlight the remarkable synergistic effects observed in the sensing performance of carbon-based nanohybrids, demonstrating the promising potential of this approach to enhance the sensitivity of such sensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gas sensor
Nanocomposites
Carbon nanotubes
Graphene
Carbon quantum dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) QCM sensor operation; (b) schematic of the sensing setup; (c) different photographs of the developed setup.
TGA curves of original and acid treated CNTs.
(a) The XRD spectrum, (b) HR-TEM image, (c) actual fluorescence spectrum, and (d) normalized fluorescence spectrum of CQDs.
TEM images of: (a), (b) Treated CNTs. (c), (d) CNT-GNP hybrid composite. (e), (f) CNT-GNP-CQD composite.
XPS survey of the different carbon-based samples. Each spectrum was normalized to 1 at the highest peak point.
Transient response of the CNT-GNP-CQD based QCM sensor to ethanol (100–7500 ppm).
Comparison of sensing performance of coated sensors detecting different concentrations of ethanol at room temperature.

Sensing performance of the CNT-GNP-CQD nanohybrid: (a) Linear fitting of its response to ethanol; (b) Response/recovery behavior towards 500 ppm ethanol; (c) Repeatability to 500 ppm ethanol repeated exposure; (d) Response to ethanol and 2-propanol.
O/C ratios obtained from the survey spectra.

head.ce:title: Novel carbon quantum dots enhanced carbon nanotubes-graphene hybrid nanocomposite for VOCs detection

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Faculty of Mechanical Engineering, Istanbul Technical University, 34437 Beyoğlu, Istanbul, Turkey.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, researchers have shown a significant interest in exploring carbon-based nanomaterials for their potential applications in sensing technologies. This study introduces the development of an innovative composite material through the integration of quantum dots with carbon nanotubes and graphene. This nanohybrid composite serves as a sensing material for detecting ethanol at room temperature. The carbon quantum dots (CQD) used in this study were synthesized through a solvothermal process and integrated with carbon nanotubes (CNT) and graphene nanoplates (GNP) to investigate the synergistic effects on the composite's structure and its sensing properties. The resulting hybrid composite was applied as a sensitive film onto the surface of a quartz crystal microbalance (QCM) sensor. The hybrid nanocomposite-based sensor demonstrated significantly enhanced sensing sensitivity. At an ethanol concentration of 500 ppm, the CQD-enhanced CNT-GNP composite exhibited approximately 10- and 15-fold higher responses compared to CNT- and GNP-coated sensors, respectively. The response/recovery times of the CQD-enhanced CNT-GNP composite sensor were approximately 2/0.5 min, respectively. The sensor maintained reasonable repeatability and good recovery. The findings highlight the remarkable synergistic effects observed in the sensing performance of carbon-based nanohybrids, demonstrating the promising potential of this approach to enhance the sensitivity of such sensors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid expansion of industrial activities has elevated air pollution to a significant concern worldwide. Emissions of toxic gases, contributing to several climate issues, pose both short and long-term threats to environmental sustainability and public health [1,2]. Common corrosive and poisonous emissions, such as nitrogen oxides, hydrogen sulfide, and ammonia, adversely impact human health and the environment. Additionally, volatile organic compounds (VOCs) are recognized as potential toxic agents and major contributors to indoor air pollution [3,4]. Ethanol, as one of these VOCs, is a vital industrial chemical used in various fields such as chemical processing, medical treatment, food production, and beverage manufacturing [5,6]. It is widely known that high concentrations of ethanol vapor are flammable and explosive when exposed to high heat or open flames [7,8]. Additionally, prolonged exposure to elevated ethanol levels can irritate the eyes and mucous membranes, and may cause dizziness, fatigue, and nausea [9,10]. Therefore, detecting and monitoring ethanol levels is crucial to safeguard public health and property. Extensive research efforts have been dedicated to developing accurate sensors with reliable sensing characteristics. The majority of existing sensing devices employ metal oxides and conducting polymers as sensing films, operating based on interactions between the material surface and the detected molecules. However, the performance of metal oxide sensors is constrained by the high operating temperatures required for an appropriate response [11], and a tendency for dense agglomerations of their nanoparticles [12]. While conductive polymers offer ease of fabrication and high sensitivity at room temperature, they come with drawbacks such as incomplete recovery, and aging in the long-term [13,14].

Recently, carbon-based nanomaterials have garnered significant interest due to their extraordinary physical and chemical properties, including a high specific surface area (SSA). This characteristic provides an excellent interaction potential, making them suitable as sensing materials [15,16]. Among various carbon-based nanomaterials, graphene and CNTs have been extensively studied [4,17–26]. Graphene consists of monolayers of sp2 carbon atoms organized in a hexagonal pattern. Common methods for obtaining graphene include mechanical exfoliation, liquid-phase exfoliation, and chemical vapor deposition (CVD) [21,26,27]. On the other hand, CNTs consist of single or multi one-atom-thick layers of sp2 carbon rolled into seamless hollow cylinders with a diameter of a few nanometers and a length of several microns [28]. Various methods, including laser ablation, arc discharge, CVD, and plasma torch, can be employed to produce CNTs with varying defect density [29–31].

Despite their potential advantages, sensors made from pristine CNTs and graphene face multiple obstacles, including low selectivity [32], poor reproducibility, and a high tendency for agglomeration [33]. Researchers have managed to address these challenges by functionalizing carbon nanomaterials, thereby enhancing the sensing characteristics. Furthermore, there has been a growing focus on exploring combinations of carbon-based nanomaterials with different geometries to assemble novel carbon nanohybrid composites. Examples include encapsulating CNTs within graphene nanoribbons [34] and inserting CNTs within graphene nanoplatelets [35,36] to investigate potential synergistic effects on their composite properties. In addition to CNTs and graphene, carbon quantum dots (CQD) have recently gained significant attention due to advances in their synthesis methods. As zero-dimensional carbonaceous nanomaterials, a few nanometers in size, CQDs exhibit unique properties such as quantum confinement, high surface functionality, and edge effects, leading to a wide range of applications [37–40]. The combination of these distinctive properties and their extremely small sizes makes CQDs a promising sensing material.

In this study, we present the synthesis of a GNP-CNT hybrid composite and its application as gas sensing films in QCM sensors for detecting various analytes at room temperature. Additionally, CQDs were synthesized using a feasible bottom-up solvothermal method, and their potential inclusion in the GNP-CNT matrix was explored to assemble a novel three-dimensional (3-D) structured carbon nanohybrid composite. A comparative investigation was conducted to assess the gas sensing capabilities of individual components, namely CNTs, GNPs, CQDs, and their hybrid composite, aiming to observe the nanohybrid's potential synergy and its effect on the overall sensing performance.

GNPs with a diameter less than 2 μm, a thickness of 3 nm, and a SSA of 750 m2/g were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich and used without any further treatment. Multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs), produced through a CVD process, with an outer diameter of 8–10 nm, length of 1–3 μm, and a SSA of 290 m2/g, were purchased from Nanografi Co. Ltd. The MWCNTs underwent further treatment by refluxing in 6 M HNO3 to purify (i.e., remove metal catalysts from the synthesized CNTs) and functionalize them. Typically, 1 g of MWCNTs was added to a flask containing 300 mL of 6 M HNO₃, and the mixture was refluxed at 120 °C for 6 h [41,42]. The resulting black solution was filtered and washed with deionized (DI) water to remove HNO3 until neutralization. Finally, the functionalized MWCNTs were dried in an oven at 105 °C for 24 h. The CQDs used in this study were synthesized through a solvothermal synthesis method using octamethylcalix[4]pyrrole as the carbon precursor [43]. First, octamethylcalix[4]pyrrole was synthesized according to literature protocols [44]. Subsequently, CQDs were synthesized as follows: 60 mg of octamethylcalix[4]pyrrole was dissolved in toluene and sealed in a Teflon-lined autoclave, which was then placed in a furnace at 200 °C for 8 h. After the synthesis, the resulting yellow crude solution was allowed to cool to ambient temperature. The crude solution underwent a two-step purification process: initially, it was filtered twice through 0.22 μm membranes, and then the filtered solution was subjected to centrifugation at 14,000 rpm to collect the supernatant. The final solution was dried at 40 °C, and the solid product was stored in a refrigerator for further use. The hybrid composites were created by dispersing 1 mg of equal ratios of these nanomaterials in a 10 mL binary solvent mixture of N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) and 2-propanol (v/v = 1:1) using a 1000 watt probe sonication for 30 min, as detailed in Table 1 . Once the carbon materials are mixed in solution, they typically bond through π-π interactions, which can induce functionalization due to the geometric differences between the various phases [45,46]. Apart from π-π interactions, covalent bonds and hydrogen bonds may also contribute to the construction of hybrid nanomaterials [45].

The impact of acidic treatment on the purification of MWCNTs was investigated through thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) using the Q600 instrument from TA Instruments. The analysis was conducted at a heating rate of 5 °C min−1, ranging from 40 °C to 800 °C, under a nitrogen flow of 100 mL/min. The effectiveness of the acidic purification treatment can be assessed by the calculating the percentage reduction in metallic impurities residues according to the following equation [42]:
(1)
Purification
%
=
W o
−
W t
W o
∗ 100
where
W o
and
W t
are the metal content (%) of original and treated CNTs, respectively. To elucidate the emission properties of the synthesized carbon quantum dots (CQDs), their photoluminescence emission spectrum was recorded by the Varian Cary-Eclipse fluorescence spectrofluorimeter, employing an excitation wavelength that varied between 350 nm and 500 nm. In the fluorescence analysis, the CQD sample was dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water after purification and diluted until achieving an optical density of 0.1 to minimize potential errors arising from self-absorption. Additionally, the CQDs were analyzed using an X-ray diffraction spectrometer (XRD, Bruker Advance D8) with Cu Kα radiation at a wavelength of 1.5406 Å and 40 kV to acquire information about their crystallographic structure. Dispersion characteristics and the internal structure of the various carbonaceous compositions were investigated with a Hitachi HighTech HT7700 transmission electron microscope (TEM). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data of the various samples were acquired using a SPECS Phoibos 100 analyzer with an Al Kα monochromatic X-ray radiation source.

In the current work, 5 MHz QCM sensors with gold electrodes were employed. Prior to applying the sensing films, the sensors were cleaned to eliminate surface contaminants. A cleaning solution, consisting of hydrogen peroxide (30 %), ammonia (25 %), and DI water in a 1:1:5 ratio, was prepared and heated to 75 °C. The QCM sensors were then soaked in this solution for 5 min. After cleaning, the sensors were washed with DI water and purged with nitrogen gas for drying. For the application of the sensing films, a high-precision micropipette was used to drop-cast 10 μL of the various carbon nanomaterials onto the QCM's electrode surface. Subsequently, the coated sensors were then allowed to dry over a 60 °C hot plate for 15 min.

The QCM sensor typically comprises a thin disc-shaped quartz crystal sandwiched between two thin metal electrodes, and its operation relies on the piezoelectric effect. This involves exciting the quartz crystal with an alternating electric current applied to the electrodes, driving the crystal to its resonance frequency. When gas molecules adsorbed onto or desorbed from the sensor's surface, a shift in its resonance frequency occurs due to a change in mass (refer to Fig. 1a). The frequency shift is inversely proportional to the mass change, and this relationship is elucidated by the Sauerbrey equation [14,47]:
(2)
∆ f = −
2
f 0 2
A
μ q
ρ q
∆ m
where ∆ f is the frequency shift of the QCM, ∆ m is the QCM mass change, A is the QCM electrode's surface area,
f 0
,
μ q
, and
ρ q
are the fundamental frequency, shear modulus, and density of QCM crystal, respectively.

In this study, a flexQCM system (FlexInst Scientific) with digital phase locked loop (PLL) based frequency shift and dissipation monitoring was used to test the coated QCM sensors. As the main goal of this study is to assess and compare the sensing abilities of various prepared sensors while eliminating the influence of ambient conditions, high-purity nitrogen (99.999 %) was used as both a carrier and balance gas in the experimental setup. Additionally, the sensor temperature within the cell was maintained at room temperature (25 °C) using a temperature controller (flexTemp, FlexInst Scientific, Turkey) positioned beneath the QCM test cell. Digital mass flow controllers, specifically MC10 and MC100 models from Alicat Scientific, were used in conjunction with a constant-temperature bubbler to generate and deliver varying concentrations of the target into a sealed 150 μL flow cell containing the QCM sensor. All setup's components are connected to a computer, and the operation and data acquisition are fully automated. The observed frequency shift from the adsorption/desorption of gas molecules was taken as the output response signal. Fig. 1b and c give a schematic representation and images of the testing setup. A set of sensing experiments was carried out to assess the sensitivity, response/recovery characteristics, and repeatability of the different prepared sensors.

The impact of nitric acid treatment on the purity of carbon nanotubes (CNT) was assessed using TGA. TGA curves of original and purified CNT are shown in Fig. 2 . The CNT treated with nitric acid exhibited fewer metallic impurities compared to the untreated CNT. According to the TGA results, the untreated CNT had 10.59 % metallic impurities. In contrast, the treated CNT showed a significant reduction to 3.154 %, indicating that nitric acid treatment was an effective technique for obtaining purer CNT samples.

The hydrophobic carbon quantum dots (CQDs) exhibited a typical X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern that has been discussed in literature before [48,49] (Fig. 3a). The pattern showed a broad peak around 20°, which can be attributed to the (002) plane of the graphitic crystal structure. The broadening of the peak was a result of the nano-sized structure of the quantum dots. The HR-TEM results were in line with XRD results. According to HR-TEM, CQDs possessed size in range of 2–5 nm (Fig. 3b). The calixpyrrole-based quantum dots contain carbon and oxygen in their structure, as extensively discussed in literature [43]. The oxygen predominantly appeared on the surface of the quantum dots, in the form of hydroxide and carboxylate, resulting in excitation dependent emission [43]. The emission peak of the CQDs red-shifted as the excitation wavelength shifted towards lower energy region, which was a consequence of presence of carboxylate and hydroxide groups on the surface (Fig. 3c,d).

TEM images of CNTs, GNPs, CQDs, and their hybrid composites are displayed in Fig. 4 . The structural integrity of CNTs remained unaltered without any signs of damage, even after undergoing a 6-h acidic treatment, as depicted in Fig. 4a, b. Additionally, the TEM images provided evidence of the successful integration between CNTs and graphene GNPs. The CNTs were observed to interconnect with GNPs, resulting in a web-like 3-D hybrid structure that may significantly enhanced the SSA of their composite (Fig. 4c, d). Furthermore, the introduction of CQDs exerted an influence on the final hybrid structure by introducing zero-dimensional roughness. This was achieved through the attachment of CQDs to the surfaces of both CNTs and GNPs (Fig. 4e, f). As a result, the surface area and active sites were further enhanced, promoting more opportunities for interactions.

XPS survey scans of the different samples were acquired and presented in Fig. 5 . The spectrum of each of the carbon-based samples involve peaks around 284 and 532 eV for C1s and O1s photoemission features, respectively. Table 2 represents the oxygen to carbon ratios of each material. The XPS results revealed that carbon is the dominant element, as anticipated. In the case of CNT, GNP, and CNT-GNP composites, the atomic percentage of carbon was 93.5 %, accompanied by oxygen (atomic percentage: 6.5 %). The presence of oxygen was attributed to oxygen containing groups attached on the surface of carbonaceous materials. However, the atomic percentage of oxygen increased to 11 % when CQDs were integrated into the structure, as expected, due to the presence of oxygen in the structure of CQDs [43]. The oxygen content in the different samples reflects the abundance of oxygen-based functional groups on their surfaces. It should be noted that calix[4]pyrrole based CQDs do not contain nitrogen in their structure. During the formation of the quantum dots, the nitrogen atoms in calix[4]pyrrole were completely removed, as discussed in details in literature before [43]. Consequently, nitrogen peaks did not appear in the XPS analysis of CQD-integrated structures in our study.

To gain a more thorough understanding of the sensing capabilities of the nanohybrid, additional gas sensing experiments were conducted with the QCM sensor incorporating the CNT-GNP-CQD film. As illustrated in Fig. 8 a, the findings demonstrated a nearly linear response to increasing ethanol concentrations within the 100–7500 ppm range. Prompt response and rapid recovery play crucial roles in effective gas devices. In this investigation of the CNT-GNP-CQD-based QCM sensor, the sensor exhibited a response time of approximately 2.05 min to reach 90 % of the steady-state response to 500 ppm of ethanol. Furthermore, the sensor required about 0.5 min returning to 10 % from the baseline frequency when the QCM cell was purged with N2 gas, as shown in Fig. 8b. After reviewing recent literature on QCM sensors based on graphene and its derivatives, which report response times ranging from 10 to 134 s and recovery times from 11 to 388 s, respectively [14,15], the hybrid composite sensor in this work demonstrates a reasonable recovery time but a relatively long response time. Furthermore, the response repeatability of the CNT-GNP-CQD hybrid was assessed by subjecting the sensor to six successive sensing cycles. Each cycle involved exposure to a 500 ppm ethanol concentration for 10 min, followed by a 10-min flushing with N2. As shown in Fig. 8c, the response profiles remained consistent across all six cycles, with only a small change in the observed response (a 6 % decrease after 2 h of cyclic exposure) and complete recovery after each cycle, indicating reasonably good reproducibility and reversibility. The CNT-GNP-CQD sensor was also assessed for its ability to detect various concentrations of 2-propanol. As showed in Fig. 8d, the sensor demonstrated lower sensitivity to 2-propanol compared to ethanol.

In this work, a hybrid composite of CNT-GNP-CQD was prepared and applied as a sensing film on a QCM sensor. The adsorption of VOC molecules onto carbonaceous materials is commonly attributed to hydrogen bonding at defect sites [50,51]. In the examined nanohybrid, polar carboxyl groups (-COOH) are present as a result of the nitric acid treatment of CNTs and the dissolved oxygen in toluene, which oxidized the surface of CQDs during high-temperature synthesis [43]. Upon exposure to ethanol, the hybrid composite exhibits sensitivity due to hydrogen bonding through dipole-dipole interactions between the COOH groups on the surface of the hybrid composite and the polar OH groups of ethanol molecules. When ethanol molecules are adsorbed on the surface of the composite over the QCM resonator, the total mass on the QCM resonator increases, resulting in a negative frequency shift according to the Sauerbrey equation [47]. At the end of exposure, when the QCM testing chamber is flushed with nitrogen, the weak hydrogen bonds between the COOH functional groups on the hybrid surface and ethanol molecules break, causing the desorption of ethanol molecules. This is evidenced by the observed complete frequency shift recovery. The proposed sensing mechanism highlights the significance of functionalities introduced during the acidic treatment of CNTs and the synthesis of CQDs in influencing the sensing performance of the final hybrid.

In summary, a novel sensor based on a hybrid nanocomposite comprising carbon quantum dots, carbon nanotubes, and graphene, integrated onto a quartz crystal microbalance (QCM) was successfully fabricated and tested. Notably, the hybrid nanocomposite-based sensor outperformed sensors coated with its individual components, revealing a significantly enhanced sensing response. Specifically, at an ethanol concentration of 500 ppm, the hybrid CNT-GNP composite exhibited a response increase of approximately 1466 % and 970 % compared to the GNP and CNT sensors, respectively. The enhanced sensing performance can be primarily attributed to the three-dimensional structure resulting from the integration of carbon-based materials with distinct geometries. This combination increased the surface area and active sites for interactions, contributing to the sensor's heightened sensitivity. The QCM sensor based on the CNT-GNP-CQD hybrid demonstrated a nearly linear sensitivity when detecting various ethanol concentrations. The CNT-GNP-CQD-based sensor exhibited a response time of approximately 2.05 min and a recovery time of 0.5 min, indicating a reasonable recovery but a relatively long response period. After 2 h of cyclic exposure to 500 ppm ethanol, the hybrid composite exhibited only a ∼6 % decrease in its initial response, revealing reasonably good repeatability. The proposed sensing mechanism involves an adsorption-desorption process facilitated by hydrogen bonding between the functional groups on the composite surface and ethanol molecules. The current study highlights the significance of exploring novel carbon structures through the integration of materials with different geometries and the resulting favorable synergistic effect on their sensing performance. This hybrid carbon-based composite holds promise for future development as a volatile organic compounds sensor. Nevertheless, further research is required to optimize the composition, evaluate the performance of the hybrid for more complex environments with a wider range of interfering gases, enhance the response characteristics, and assess its long-term stability.

Mohamad-Anas Hejazi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. İbrahim Yağız Coşkun: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Fatma Yelda Ünlü: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Abdullah Aydogan: Visualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Caner Ünlü: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Levent Trabzon: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the funding from The Scientific and Technological Research Council of Turkey (TUBITAK) - Project No. 120N084.
